title: Spiral Matrix
description: Given an `m x n` matrix, return all elements of the matrix in spiral order.
whatShouldHappen: Traverse the matrix in a clockwise spiral direction
Maintain boundaries for top, bottom, left, and right
Return a flat array of all elements in the traversed order
skeletonHint: Use four boundaries: `top`, `bottom`, `left`, and `right`. Increment/decrement these boundaries as you traverse each side of the matrix in a clockwise direction. Be careful to check `top <= bottom` and `left <= right` before traversing left and up.
patternExplanation: The problem is solved by simulating the clockwise traversal process, keeping track of boundaries to know when to turn and when the traversal is complete.

title: Set Matrix Zeroes
description:
Given an `m x n` integer matrix `matrix`, if an element is `0`, set its entire row and column to `0`'s. You must do it **in place**.

### Examples

**Input:** `matrix = [[1,1,1],[1,0,1],[1,1,1]]`  
**Output:** `[[1,0,1],[0,0,0],[1,0,1]]`  

**Input:** `matrix = [[0,1,2,0],[3,4,5,2],[1,3,1,5]]`  
**Output:** `[[0,0,0,0],[0,4,5,0],[0,3,1,0]]`
whatShouldHappen: Identify all rows and columns that contain at least one zero
Update the matrix in-place so that those entire rows and columns are zeroed
Use O(1) extra space if possible (by using the first row and column as markers)
skeletonHint: To achieve O(1) space, use the first row and first column of the matrix itself to store whether a row or column should be zeroed. You'll need two boolean variables to separately track if the very first row and first column originally contained any zeros.
patternExplanation: This problem uses the matrix itself to store state markers to achieve optimal space complexity. We use the first row and column to flag other rows/cols, then handle the first row/col separately.

title: Search a 2D Matrix
description:
Write an efficient algorithm that searches for a value `target` in an `m x n` integer matrix `matrix`. This matrix has the following properties:

1. Integers in each row are sorted from left to right.
2. The first integer of each row is greater than the last integer of the previous row.

### Examples

**Input:** `matrix = [[1,3,5,7],[10,11,16,20],[23,30,34,60]], target = 3`  
**Output:** `true`  

**Input:** `matrix = [[1,3,5,7],[10,11,16,20],[23,30,34,60]], target = 13`  
**Output:** `false`
whatShouldHappen: Treat the 2D matrix as a virtual 1D sorted array
Perform a standard binary search on this virtual array
Map the 1D index back to 2D coordinates `(row = idx / cols, col = idx % cols)`
skeletonHint: Since the matrix is fully sorted (as if it were a single 1D array), you can use binary search. To convert a 1D index `i` into 2D coordinates for an `m x n` matrix: `row = Math.floor(i / n)` and `col = i % n`.
patternExplanation: The matrix properties allow us to treat the entire structure as one continuous sorted array, making binary search the most efficient O(log(m*n)) approach.

title: Word Search
description:
Given an `m x n` grid of characters `board` and a string `word`, return `true` if `word` exists in the grid. 

The word can be constructed from letters of sequentially adjacent cells, where adjacent cells are horizontally or vertically neighboring. The same letter cell may not be used more than once in a single word path.

### Examples

**Input:** `board = [["A","B","C","E"],["S","F","C","S"],["A","D","E","E"]], word = "ABCCED"`  
**Output:** `true`  

**Input:** `board = [["A","B","C","E"],["S","F","C","S"],["A","D","E","E"]], word = "SEE"`  
**Output:** `true`  

**Input:** `board = [["A","B","C","E"],["S","F","C","S"],["A","D","E","E"]], word = "ABCB"`  
**Output:** `false`
whatShouldHappen: Iterate through every cell in the grid to find a starting character match
From each match, use Depth First Search (DFS) to explore neighbors (Up, Down, Left, Right)
Mark cells as visited during the DFS to avoid reuse, then unmark them (Backtracking)
skeletonHint: Use a helper function for Depth First Search (DFS). When checking a cell, ensure it's within bounds and matches the current character of the word. Temporarily mark the cell (e.g., by changing its value to a special character) to avoid using it twice in the same path, and restore it before returning (backtracking).
patternExplanation: We explore all possible paths using DFS and backtrack when a path doesn't lead to the solution, ensuring each cell is only used once per path.

title: Word Search II
description:
Given an `m x n` `board` of characters and a list of strings `words`, return *all words on the board*.

Each word must be constructed from letters of sequentially adjacent cells, where **adjacent cells** are horizontally or vertically neighboring. The same letter cell may not be used more than once in a word.

### Examples

**Input:** `board = [["o","a","a","n"],["e","t","a","e"],["i","h","k","r"],["i","f","l","v"]], words = ["oath","pea","eat","rain"]`  
**Output:** `["eat","oath"]`
whatShouldHappen: Build a Trie of all input words to allow simultaneous searching
Use Backtracking (DFS) from each cell on the board to find words in the Trie
Remove found words from the Trie to avoid duplicates and improve performance (Pruning)
skeletonHint: First, insert all `words` into a Trie. Then, iterate through every cell on the board and start a DFS. If the character exists as a child of the current Trie node, continue exploring neighbors. If a Trie node has a `word` property, you've found a word!
patternExplanation: Using a Trie allows us to prune the search space significantly. Instead of searching for each word individually (Word Search I style), we can check all words simultaneously as we traverse the board.

title: Rotate Image
description:
You are given an `n x n` 2D matrix representing an image, rotate the image by 90 degrees (clockwise).

You have to rotate the image **in-place**, which means you have to modify the input 2D matrix directly. **DO NOT** allocate another 2D matrix and do the rotation.
whatShouldHappen: The input matrix should be modified in-place.
The matrix should be rotated 90 degrees clockwise.
The function should return the modified matrix.
skeletonHint: A 90-degree clockwise rotation can be achieved by first transposing the matrix (swapping `matrix[i][j]` with `matrix[j][i]`) and then reversing each row.
patternExplanation: Rotating a matrix in-place often involves a combination of transposing (flipping over the diagonal) and reflecting (reversing rows or columns).
